profile.summary: Owns reliability across multiple services or a platform area, balancing hands-on engineering with cross-team influence. Sets standards for availability, incident response, observability, and operational readiness. Partners with software engineers, platform teams, security, and engineering managers to reduce risk, improve resilience, and guide how teams run production systems.

known_challenges.text: Carrying broad reliability responsibility without direct authority over every service team.
Switching between strategic prevention work and urgent incident or escalation support.
Driving adoption of SLOs, runbooks, and observability practices across teams with different priorities.
Managing on-call load and after-hours interruptions while still expected to deliver long-term improvements.
Pushing back on risky launches or weak operational readiness without being seen as blocking delivery.

recommended_question_themes.why: This role often sees more reliability risk than any single product team and needs help focusing where influence matters most.
Senior SREs are most effective when they can reduce toil and prevent repeat issues instead of living in constant reaction mode.
Much of the role depends on getting product and engineering teams to adopt better practices they do not directly own.
The value of this role is not only handling incidents but making sure learning turns into concrete prevention work.
Even experienced SREs can burn down if escalation paths, rotations, and recovery time are not realistic.

terminology.meaning: Allowed unreliability before delivery should slow down.
Seeing system health through metrics, logs, and traces.
Step-by-step guide for common operational tasks.
Manual repetitive operational work with low long-term value.
Person coordinating response during an incident.
Blameless review of what happened and what changes.
Scheduled responsibility for production issues and alerts.
Routing a problem to higher expertise or ownership.
Whether a service is prepared to run safely in production.
Too many noisy alerts reduce response quality.
Cross-team check of risks, dependencies, and safeguards.
Forecasting resources needed to handle future load.
Ability to keep working through failures or recover fast.
Clear responsibility for running and improving a service.
Standards or automation that prevent risky changes.

listen_for: Whether they talk about reducing repeat incidents, not just responding faster.
Whether they name cross-team dependencies and ownership gaps clearly.
Shifts from deep technical detail into influence, standards, and tradeoff language.
Signs they are absorbing too many escalations personally.
Whether they describe alerts and incidents in terms of user impact, not only system symptoms.
avoid: Treating them like the team that just fixes production problems for everyone else.
Assuming senior means they should absorb unhealthy on-call or escalation load.
Pushing feature speed without making space for reliability work and follow-through.
Reading careful risk management as negativity or resistance to delivery.
